headline: Full-stack engineer with 3 shipped fintech products and a deep interest in systems that handle real money
profile_summary: Maya is the kind of engineer who thinks about edge cases before she writes the first line of code. She's spent the last three years building financial infrastructure at two early-stage startups, and she describes her superpower as turning ambiguous product requirements into reliable, auditable systems. She's motivated by problems that actually matter — she's said explicitly that she wants her work to connect to something real, not just move metrics on a dashboard. She prefers small, ownership-heavy teams and is skeptical of process for its own sake. She's based in Brooklyn, works remotely, and is looking for her first role at a company past Series B.
fit_summary: Maya has direct, production-grade experience building the exact type of financial infrastructure this role requires. Her conversation history with Dilly reveals strong alignment with the team's culture preferences and a genuine interest in the domain — this isn't a resume keyword match, it's a real fit.
fit_narrative: Maya's experience maps closely to this role across multiple dimensions. She built a ledger reconciliation system at her last company that handled $2M in daily transaction volume — the same class of problem this team is working on. Her conversation with Dilly revealed she has thought carefully about idempotency, distributed consistency, and audit trails — which are the core concerns in the job description. Beyond the technical fit, Maya's stated preferences around team culture, ownership model, and company stage align with what's described in the role. She's not applying because the tech stack matches. She's applying because the problem matches.
top_gap: No experience with the specific compliance frameworks (SOC 2 Type II audit process)

Her financial systems experience is directly relevant, her culture preferences match your team, and she clearly cares about the domain for real reasons. The compliance gap is the only hesitation, and it's narrow. I'd move her to a phone screen this week.

headline: Product-minded frontend engineer who's shipped 4 consumer apps and thinks deeply about the gap between what's built and what users actually do
profile_summary: Jordan came to software engineering through design, and that path is visible in everything he ships. He thinks about user flows before he thinks about implementation, and he's unusually good at pushing back on product requirements when the interaction model is broken. He's spent three years at consumer tech companies and has a track record of catching usability issues in code review that wouldn't surface until QA. He's thoughtful about his own growth in a way that's rare — he can articulate exactly where he's strong, where he's weak, and what he's deliberately working on. He's looking for a team where design and engineering work closely together and where 'good enough' isn't the default.
fit_summary: Jordan's product sensibility and frontend depth are exactly what this role requires. His background in consumer apps translates directly to the user-facing complexity described in the job description, and his conversation history shows he's already thought carefully about the specific challenges this product faces.
fit_narrative: Jordan is a rare engineer: technically strong and product-minded in equal measure. His portfolio includes 4 shipped consumer products — not side projects, production apps with real users. In his Dilly conversations, he described the challenge of building interfaces that work under real-world conditions (poor connectivity, interruption, edge-case user behavior) in a way that suggests he's solved this problem before. His stated preference for design-engineering collaboration and his frustration with teams that treat UX as a last step both point toward strong culture fit. The one area to probe: he's strongest in React/TypeScript and has limited native mobile experience, which may matter depending on how the team divides platform work.
top_gap: Limited native mobile experience (iOS/Android) — primarily React/TypeScript focused

dilly_take: Jordan is the kind of engineer who makes design-to-engineering handoffs better just by being in the room. His consumer app experience is real, his product thinking is exceptional, and his self-awareness about his own gaps (mobile, architecture DRI) suggests he'll ramp fast on the things he doesn't know yet. Move him forward.

headline: ML engineer who builds models that actually deploy — 3 years bridging research and production across healthcare and climate tech
profile_summary: Priya has a frustration she talks about often: most ML work never ships. She spent two years watching promising research models die in staging environments because nobody thought about inference latency, data pipeline reliability, or the ops burden on the team maintaining them. So she retooled. She's now as comfortable with MLOps and production deployment as she is with model development, and she's proud of that. She's motivated by impact — she wants to be able to point at something and say the model she built is running, it's making decisions, and it's better than the human baseline. She's been explicit that she's not interested in research for its own sake. She's pragmatic, self-directed, and unusually good at translating between technical and product stakeholders.
fit_summary: Priya's combination of modeling depth and production deployment experience is rare and directly relevant. Her specific frustration with 'ML that doesn't ship' suggests she's already solved the problem this team is tackling, and her communication skills mean she'll work well with the non-technical stakeholders the role requires.
fit_narrative: This is a strong fit with a notable depth advantage. Most ML candidates at Priya's level are strong in one of two areas: modeling or deployment. Priya is strong in both, and she's built the combination deliberately because she's frustrated by the gap. Her healthcare ML experience is directly relevant to the domain, and her 3 production deployments — all in regulated industries — mean she understands the compliance and reliability constraints that make this work hard. The one area to probe is team leadership: she's led projects but not people, and this role has a 'tech lead' component that will require mentoring junior engineers.
top_gap: No experience managing or mentoring direct reports — has led projects but not people

She's solved the research-to-production gap deliberately, she understands regulated environments, and she communicates well. The tech lead question is real — but it's a question worth asking, not a reason to skip her.

headline: Backend engineer transitioning from enterprise to startup — strong systems foundation, early in domain-specific depth
profile_summary: Sam spent five years at a large enterprise software company writing distributed systems that processed hundreds of millions of events per day. He's technically sharp and has a strong systems foundation, but he's been honest with Dilly that he spent most of that time in a highly structured environment with clearly defined requirements, slow release cycles, and extensive QA processes. He wants something different — faster iteration, more ownership, closer to the product. He's in the early stages of that transition and knows it. He's described himself as someone who learns fast once he has a real problem to dig into, and his side project work supports that self-assessment. The motivation is genuine. The experience gap in startup-paced environments is real.
fit_summary: Sam has the technical foundation this role requires, but his enterprise background means he's still developing the judgment that comes from fast-iteration, high-ownership startup environments. Genuine motivation to make this transition — but the ramp may take longer than this team can absorb right now.
fit_narrative: Sam is a technically credible candidate whose experience creates a specific profile: very strong on systems fundamentals (distributed architecture, performance at scale, reliability engineering), weaker on the startup-specific skills that come from shipping fast under ambiguity. His Dilly conversations reveal honest self-awareness about this gap — he's not claiming to be something he isn't. The question for this team is whether the role has room for a 3-6 month ramp period. His systems foundation is genuinely strong, and his side projects (a real-time analytics tool he built solo over 6 months) suggest he learns effectively when motivated. But the judgment that comes from shipping in a startup environment is earned through experience, not just talent.
top_gap: Limited experience in fast-iteration startup environments — 5 years in enterprise with slower release cycles

headline: Data engineer with strong analytics foundation looking to move closer to real-time systems and product infrastructure
profile_summary: Alex is intellectually curious and technically solid, with four years of experience building the kinds of data pipelines that analytics teams depend on. She's good at her work, but she's been direct with Dilly: she feels like she's building the plumbing for other people's decisions instead of being close to the product herself. She wants to move toward infrastructure that's closer to the product surface — real-time systems, APIs, the things that users actually touch. She has the data systems fundamentals. She's in the process of developing the backend engineering breadth the transition requires. Her profile shows someone with strong learning velocity and honest self-assessment about where she is in that journey.
fit_summary: Alex's data engineering background gives her strong foundations in pipeline design, data modeling, and systems reliability. The gap is in backend API development and real-time systems — areas she's actively developing. High potential candidate who would need structured development support to reach full productivity in this role.
fit_narrative: Alex has the data systems foundation and the intellectual profile to succeed in this role, but she's mid-transition between data engineering and backend engineering. Her strengths (pipeline architecture, data modeling, SQL optimization, reliability thinking) are directly applicable to parts of the role. Her gaps (REST API design, service architecture, real-time systems) are the other parts. The question is whether your team can invest in filling the gaps over 4-6 months. Her Dilly conversations suggest she's a fast learner when motivated, and her motivation here is genuine — she's been thinking about this transition for 18 months and has been deliberately building toward it.
top_gap: Limited REST API and service architecture experience — strong in data pipelines, developing on backend services

headline: Security engineer with 4 years in application security and a growing focus on secure-by-design systems architecture
profile_summary: Casey grew up in bug bounty culture before it was mainstream, which gives him an attacker's mindset that most security engineers don't develop until much later in their careers. He's spent four years translating that instinct into a professional practice — penetration testing, threat modeling, secure code review. He's now interested in the upstream problem: how do you build systems that are hard to break in the first place, rather than testing them after they're built? He's making a deliberate move from traditional AppSec toward security architecture. He's intellectually rigorous, genuinely curious, and unusually good at explaining security concepts to non-security engineers in ways that change behavior rather than just creating compliance theater.
fit_summary: Casey's application security depth is strong and directly relevant. The gap is in cloud infrastructure security and the shift from reactive AppSec to proactive security architecture — he's deliberately moving in that direction but is mid-transition. Strong candidate if the role values AppSec depth over pure cloud security breadth.
fit_narrative: Casey brings a genuinely unusual combination: attacker-mindset security thinking developed through real bug bounty experience, combined with the professional discipline of an AppSec practitioner. His threat modeling and secure code review skills are strong and applicable to this role. The gap is in cloud-native security architecture — AWS security groups, IAM design, VPC architecture, infrastructure-as-code security — which the JD lists as important. Casey has exposure to these areas but not depth. His Dilly conversations show he's aware of this gap and building toward it. The question is whether this team needs depth in cloud security immediately, or whether strong AppSec foundation with cloud security developing is an acceptable profile.
top_gap: Cloud infrastructure security depth (AWS IAM, VPC design, infrastructure security) is developing, not production-proven

headline: Recent computer science graduate with strong academic foundations and limited production experience
profile_summary: Jamie graduated last spring with a strong GPA and some genuinely impressive academic projects — an implementation of a distributed key-value store, a real-time collaborative editor prototype, and a machine learning project that got into a workshop at NeurIPS. He's smart and he knows it, but he's been honest with Dilly that he's not sure what kind of engineer he wants to be yet. He's exploring broadly, which is appropriate for someone who graduated 8 months ago, but it means his profile doesn't point clearly at any specific role type. He hasn't shipped a production system, he hasn't worked on a team with real code review practices, and he hasn't experienced the parts of software engineering that aren't in textbooks: stakeholder management, technical debt, the cost of long-term maintenance decisions. He's a high-ceiling candidate who isn't ready for a senior role.
fit_summary: Jamie has academic promise but lacks the production experience this role requires. The gap isn't about intelligence — it's about the judgment that comes from shipping software in real environments with real constraints. Not the right fit for this specific role, but worth watching for earlier-stage or rotational opportunities.
fit_narrative: The honest assessment: Jamie isn't ready for this role. The role requires production-grade backend development experience, cross-functional collaboration skills developed through real projects, and judgment that comes from making decisions under real constraints. Jamie has none of those things yet — not through any failing, but because he graduated 8 months ago. His academic work is genuinely impressive for his level, and the distributed systems course project shows technical ambition. But academic projects and production systems are different in ways that matter: error handling, monitoring, oncall, refactoring other people's code, explaining technical tradeoffs to a product manager. The gap is too large for this specific role to absorb.
top_gap: No production software engineering experience — all work is academic projects or personal side projects

headline: DevOps and platform engineer with 6 years building the infrastructure layer that lets product teams ship without thinking about infrastructure
profile_summary: Dana has a clear philosophy: the best infrastructure is invisible to the people who depend on it. She's spent six years building developer platforms, CI/CD pipelines, and observability systems at companies ranging from 20 to 2,000 engineers, and she measures her success by how rarely developers have to think about the infrastructure she built. She's deeply technical and deeply collaborative — she's said she thinks of herself as serving the engineering teams she works with, not owning territory. She's particularly strong on the operational side: she's been oncall for production systems with aggressive SLAs and knows what it takes to build something that holds up at 3am. She's looking for a company where infrastructure is valued as a product, not treated as a cost center.
fit_summary: Dana's platform engineering experience maps directly to what this role requires. Her philosophy of infrastructure-as-a-product aligns precisely with the team's positioning, and her experience across multiple company sizes means she brings the judgment to build for where the company is going, not just where it is.
fit_narrative: Dana is a strong fit along multiple dimensions. Her technical experience with Kubernetes, Terraform, and observability systems is exactly the stack this role requires, and she's used all of it in production at relevant scale. More importantly, her philosophy about infrastructure — that it should be invisible to product engineers — matches the team's stated goal of building a platform that removes friction from the development process. Her cross-company experience (startup to mid-stage growth) means she understands how infrastructure needs to evolve as a company scales, which is the specific challenge this team is navigating. The only gap to probe is whether she has experience with the specific compliance requirements the company faces.
top_gap: Limited direct experience with SOC 2 and enterprise compliance requirements

dilly_take: Dana is the kind of platform engineer who will make your product teams move faster without them knowing why. Her track record is excellent, her philosophy matches your team's goals, and she's the kind of person who builds for the future, not just the present. Move her forward.

headline: Product manager with 5 years in B2B SaaS pivoting to technical PM — strong product instincts, developing technical depth
profile_summary: Morgan has spent five years doing product work that he's proud of, but he's been honest with Dilly about a growing frustration: he can articulate what needs to be built better than he can evaluate whether what's been built is right. He's decided to close that gap. He's been taking evening courses in software architecture, working through side projects in Python, and specifically seeking out opportunities to work with highly technical teams where he can develop better engineering judgment. He's a strong communicator, a good writer, and — according to multiple engineers he's worked with — unusually good at translating between business requirements and technical constraints. He's not trying to become an engineer. He's trying to become a PM who engineers respect, not just tolerate.
fit_summary: Morgan's product instincts and communication skills are strong. The technical PM depth the role requires is still developing. High potential if the role can operate at a 'strong product fundamentals, developing technical depth' level for the first 6-9 months.
fit_narrative: Morgan is in an interesting position: his product skills are strong and his technical skills are developing in exactly the right direction. The question is whether the technical PM requirements of this role are blockers or stretch goals. His track record on the product side is strong — 3 shipped B2B products, all hitting their core success metrics. His engineering relationships are unusually good for a PM (multiple engineers have described him as the PM who 'actually understands what I'm saying'). The gap: he hasn't owned a technical product domain end-to-end, hasn't been in rooms where architecture decisions get made, and hasn't developed the system-level intuition that the most technical PM roles require. This is developing, but it's 12+ months away from being demonstrated.
top_gap: Technical PM depth — has strong product fundamentals but limited architectural judgment and system-level decision-making experience

headline: Senior iOS engineer with 7 years building consumer apps at scale — shipped 3 apps that crossed 1M users
profile_summary: Riley is one of those engineers who has a strong opinion about everything, expressed clearly and without apology. She thinks bad mobile UX is a form of disrespect to users, and she's made decisions in code review that she knew would slow down shipping because she wasn't willing to ship something she'd be embarrassed by. That's cost her some friction with PMs, but it's also produced a track record of apps that users actually love — 3 apps above 1M installs, one with a 4.9 App Store rating sustained over 2 years. She's deeply technical on the iOS side and increasingly curious about the system design decisions that happen above the client layer. She's looking for a team where mobile is a first-class investment, not an afterthought, and where she'll have the authority to make decisions about the product surface that users actually touch.
fit_summary: Riley's combination of technical depth, product sensibility, and track record of shipping beloved consumer apps makes her an exceptional match for this role. Her opinions about mobile quality are features, not bugs — they're exactly what a product that cares about user experience needs.
fit_narrative: Riley is a strong fit on every dimension this role cares about. Her iOS technical depth is production-proven across 7 years and 3 apps at significant scale, including one that's maintained a 4.9 App Store rating for two years — a genuinely hard thing to sustain. Her product sensibility is strong and grounded in conviction, not just feedback loops. Her Dilly conversations reveal a person who thinks about the gap between what engineers consider 'working' and what users consider 'good' — which is the exact orientation this product needs at this stage of its development. The main gap to probe is her experience with the specific infrastructure constraints on the server side, since this role has a cross-functional component.
top_gap: Limited cross-platform experience (Android/React Native) — exclusively iOS-focused throughout career

profile_depth_note: 5 Dilly conversations, 51 profile facts. Highly opinionated and articulate — gives excellent signal on what she wants and why. One of the most revealing profiles in the pool.
dilly_take: Riley will make your iOS product significantly better. She has strong opinions and she's usually right — user testing data backs her up. If your team values craft and has the patience for someone who won't ship something she isn't proud of, Riley is exceptional. If you need fast-and-loose shipping culture, she's the wrong fit. But for a team that wants to build something users love, she's exactly right.
